name: MiniMax-M3
description: MiniMax's natively multimodal 428B MoE (23B active) with a 1M-token context via MiniMax Sparse Attention, launched June 2026 with weights on Hugging Face by 2026-06-07. Vendor reports 80.5% SWE-bench Verified; Artificial Analysis scores it 44 on Intelligence Index v4.1. Unlike MIT-licensed M2, M3 ships under a MiniMax Community License, and training code and some inference operators are withheld.

evidence.value: SWE-bench Verified 80.5%, SWE-bench Pro 59.0%, Terminal-Bench 2.1 66%, SWE-fficiency 34.8, KernelBench Hard 28.8
Frontier claims flagged as vendor-run on MiniMax's own infrastructure with vendor-selected baselines

task_accuracy_code.methodology: Vendor benchmarks with independent scrutiny noting all launch figures were vendor-run; partial independent corroboration via Artificial Analysis

evidence.value: Reasoning modes (enabled/adaptive/disabled) via API; competitive reasoning for a 23B-active footprint

task_accuracy_reasoning.methodology: Vendor-reported reasoning benchmarks and early community evaluation

evidence.value: Intelligence Index v4.1 score of 44 — well above the open-weight median (25), tied with DeepSeek V4 Pro, behind GLM-5.2 (51); roughly level with Claude Sonnet 4.6 on GDPval-AA agentic benchmark
MMMU Pro 78.1, Video-MME v2 85.4 — strong native multimodal understanding

task_accuracy_general.methodology: Independent composite benchmarking (Artificial Analysis) plus vendor multimodal benchmarks

evidence.value: Early agentic evaluations report capable but mixed consistency across long workflows; limited observation window since June launch

output_consistency.methodology: Early community testing of repeated runs and agentic trajectories

evidence.value: MiniMax Sparse Attention delivers 9x prefill and 15x decode speedups vs M2 at 1M context, cutting per-token compute to 1/20; 23B active keeps generation fast

latency_p50.methodology: Median latency for API requests with standard prompt sizes; architecture-level efficiency verified in vendor technical report (arXiv:2606.13392)

evidence.value: p95 ~4.5s; very long multimodal or 1M-context requests run longer

latency_p95.methodology: 95th percentile response time from early third-party measurements

evidence.value: 1M-token context window enabled by MiniMax Sparse Attention
First-party API stable in first weeks, with a paid Priority tier (1.5x) for admission and latency guarantees; no long-run availability record yet

uptime.methodology: Review of platform availability since launch; observation window under six weeks

performance_reliability.notes: Meaningful step over M2: 1M context, native multimodality, and independently confirmed intelligence gains (Artificial Analysis 44 vs open-weight median 25). Headline coding figures (80.5% SWE-bench Verified) are vendor-run on vendor infrastructure and drew explicit independent skepticism at launch; GLM-5.2 leads it on independent indices.

evidence.value: Safety tuning described; no published third-party prompt-injection audit; multimodal inputs (image/video) widen the injection surface

prompt_injection_resistance.methodology: Review of vendor documentation against OWASP LLM01 patterns; model too new for mature red-team coverage

evidence.value: Standard alignment tuning; open weights allow guardrail removal in derivatives; limited adversarial testing published since launch

jailbreak_resistance.methodology: Early testing against adversarial prompt datasets; deployer-dependent for self-hosted use

evidence.value: Standard data handling on first-party API; full control when self-hosted

data_leakage_prevention.methodology: Analysis of privacy policies and self-hosting data-control options

evidence.value: Safety post-training applied; refusal behavior in line with peer open models per early reports

output_safety.methodology: Safety testing across harmful content categories per vendor card and early community reports

evidence.value: API key authentication, HTTPS only, rate limiting; OpenAI- and Anthropic-compatible endpoints carried over from M2

api_security.methodology: Review of API security features and best practices

security.notes: Standard open-model posture without third-party audits, scored slightly below M2 for launch recency and the wider multimodal attack surface. Self-hosting shifts security responsibility to the deployer.

data_residency.value: China (first-party MiniMax API); any jurisdiction when self-hosted or via Western hosts

evidence.value: MiniMax is a China-based provider; first-party API data processed under Chinese jurisdiction
Open weights served by Western inference providers, enabling non-China residency (subject to Community License terms)

data_residency.methodology: Review of provider jurisdiction and third-party hosting options

evidence.value: Standard API data terms; self-hosting removes the concern entirely

training_data_optout.methodology: Analysis of privacy policy and data usage terms

data_retention.value: Per MiniMax policy on first-party API (China jurisdiction); zero when self-hosted

evidence.value: First-party retention governed by Chinese data regulations; self-hosted deployments retain nothing externally

data_retention.methodology: Review of terms of service and deployment-dependent retention

evidence.value: Customer responsible for PII redaction; no managed PII tooling; multimodal inputs (images/video) add PII-handling complexity

pii_handling.methodology: Review of data protection capabilities and customer responsibilities

evidence.value: No published SOC 2 / HIPAA / GDPR attestations for the first-party API

compliance_certifications.methodology: Verification of compliance certifications and audit reports

evidence.value: Self-hosting (vLLM/SGLang/Transformers/KTransformers) gives complete data control and zero external retention; Community License conditions apply to commercial deployments

zero_data_retention.methodology: Review of self-hosting deployment options enabling zero retention

privacy_compliance.notes: First-party MiniMax API operates under Chinese jurisdiction — a material caveat for Western regulated industries. Self-hosting mitigates residency, but unlike MIT-licensed M2, M3's Community License attaches attribution and revenue-threshold conditions to commercial self-hosted use.

evidence.value: Reasoning modes (enabled/adaptive/disabled) expose or suppress thinking traces per request, aiding agent-loop auditability

explainability.methodology: Evaluation of reasoning transparency and trajectory inspectability

evidence.value: Early agentic evaluations report moderate hallucination; tool-grounded workflows perform better than closed-book QA

hallucination_rate.methodology: Early testing on factual QA and tool-augmented workflows

evidence.value: Limited published bias evaluation, including for multimodal inputs

bias_fairness.methodology: Review of published bias benchmarks and community evaluations

evidence.value: Basic uncertainty expression; no calibrated confidence outputs; limited testing since launch

uncertainty_quantification.methodology: Qualitative assessment of confidence expression in outputs

evidence.source: Hugging Face model card and MSA technical report
evidence.value: Clear documentation of 428B/23B MoE, MSA architecture (arXiv:2606.13392), benchmark tables, recommended inference parameters, and deployment guidance

model_card_quality.methodology: Review of documentation completeness and clarity

evidence.value: Training code, data pipelines, and specific inference operators withheld; data mixture not published — a step back from full open-source transparency
Analysis of what MiniMax disclosed vs withheld about M3's training data and pipeline

training_data_transparency.methodology: Review of public disclosures about training data and released artifacts

evidence.value: Built-in safety tuning; deployers of open weights must layer their own guardrails, including for image/video inputs

trust_transparency.notes: Good architectural documentation (MSA technical report) but reduced openness vs M2: training code, data pipelines, and some inference operators are withheld, and all launch benchmarks were vendor-run — independent reviewers explicitly flagged the verification gap.

evidence.value: OpenAI- and Anthropic-compatible endpoints with streaming, tool calling, reasoning modes, prompt caching, and a Priority service tier

api_design_quality.methodology: Review of API design, consistency, and feature completeness

evidence.value: Compatibility with mainstream OpenAI/Anthropic SDKs; first-party tooling adequate but M3-specific tooling still young

sdk_quality.methodology: Review of SDK quality, documentation, and maintenance

evidence.value: M3 announced 2026-06-01 with weights on Hugging Face by 2026-06-07; M2 weights remain available; license changed from MIT (M2) to MiniMax Community License (M3) between generations

versioning_policy.methodology: Review of versioning practices, weight availability, and license continuity across releases

evidence.value: Basic usage dashboard; self-hosted observability is deployer-built

monitoring_observability.methodology: Review of available monitoring tools and metrics

evidence.value: GitHub and community support; limited English-language enterprise support

support_quality.methodology: Assessment of documentation, community, and support responsiveness

evidence.value: Day-one vLLM/SGLang/Transformers/KTransformers/unsloth support with community quantizations and OpenRouter availability; ~259K Hugging Face downloads in the first month, though the M3-specific ecosystem is only weeks old

ecosystem_maturity.methodology: Analysis of third-party hosting, integrations, and tooling; conservative given launch recency

evidence.value: Not MIT/Apache: commercial use requires a visible 'Built with MiniMax M3' notice, and products exceeding the license's yearly revenue threshold need separate authorization
'Available to download' does not mean 'free for any commercial use' — reviewers advise reading the license before building commercial products on M3

license_terms.methodology: Review of licensing terms and restrictions; regression from M2's clean MIT license is trust-relevant

operational_excellence.notes: Dual OpenAI/Anthropic compatibility and broad day-one inference support carry over from M2, but the license regression (MIT to MiniMax Community License with attribution and revenue-threshold clauses) and withheld training/inference code lower the operational score. Commercial deployers need legal review.

code-generation.notes: Vendor-reported 80.5% SWE-bench Verified and 59.0% SWE-bench Pro at very low cost, but figures are vendor-run and GLM-5.2 leads on independent indices.

customer-support.notes: Fast (23B active), very cheap, and multimodal — can handle screenshot- and image-based support tickets natively.

content-creation.notes: Good generation quality at minimal cost; image/video understanding aids content workflows.

data-analysis.notes: Good tool calling plus native chart/image understanding; weaker raw reasoning than GLM-5.2 or Kimi K2.6.

research-assistant.notes: 1M context and native multimodality (text/image/video) suit long-document and mixed-media research at low cost.

legal-compliance.notes: China-jurisdiction first-party API, no Western certifications, and Community License conditions make this a poor fit without careful mitigation.

healthcare.notes: Not recommended via first-party API; self-hosted deployment in a compliant environment is the only viable path, with license review.

financial-analysis.notes: Capable and cheap with chart/document understanding; data residency requires self-hosting for regulated firms.

education.notes: Multimodal tutoring (diagrams, video) at very low cost suits high-volume educational platforms.

creative-writing.notes: Serviceable creative output; not its design focus.

strengths: Native multimodality: mixed-modality training from step one across text, image, and video (78.1 MMMU Pro, 85.4 Video-MME v2)
1M-token context with MiniMax Sparse Attention: 9x prefill / 15x decode speedups vs M2 at 1M context
Efficient 23B-active footprint keeps inference fast and self-hosting relatively affordable
Aggressive pricing: $0.30/$1.20 per 1M tokens (promotional 50% off list) with $0.06 cache reads
Independently confirmed intelligence gain: Artificial Analysis 44, well above the open-weight median
limitations: License regression from M2: MiniMax Community License requires 'Built with MiniMax M3' attribution and separate authorization above a revenue threshold
Training code, data pipelines, and some inference operators withheld despite the 'open-weight' label
All launch benchmarks vendor-run on vendor infrastructure; independent reviewers flagged the verification gap
First-party API processes data under Chinese jurisdiction with no published Western compliance certifications
Behind GLM-5.2 on independent intelligence and agentic indices
Long-context pricing doubles above 512K input tokens
Launched June 2026 — consistency, uptime, and security evidence still immature
best_for: Multimodal agentic workloads mixing text, screenshots, documents, and video at low cost
1M-context document and codebase analysis on a budget
High-volume workloads where the 23B-active footprint's speed and price dominate
Teams already on M2 wanting multimodality and longer context with API compatibility
not_recommended_for: Regulated Western workloads (healthcare, legal, finance) on the first-party API
Commercial products unwilling to carry 'Built with MiniMax M3' attribution or negotiate above the revenue threshold
Teams requiring vendor-independent benchmark verification before adoption
Workloads needing the strongest open-weight coding performance (GLM-5.2 leads independently)

pricing.notes: Promotional 'permanent 50% off' from list $0.60/$2.40. Rates double above 512K input tokens; Priority tier is 1.5x for admission/latency guarantees. Third-party host pricing varies.

metadata.license: MiniMax Community License (attribution notice required for commercial use; separate authorization above yearly revenue threshold)
metadata.architecture: Mixture-of-Experts: ~428B total / ~23B active parameters, MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA), native mixed-modality training (arXiv:2606.13392)
